title: Query Parameters in Action
subtitle: Customize what you get back by adding options to the URL

sections.content:
Query parameters are how you **customize** what a server returns.

They go after a `?` in the URL. Each one is `key=value`. Multiple ones are separated by `&`.

The server reads them like options on a form — filter by this, sort by that, limit to N results.
keyPoints: The server defines what each parameter means — you can't invent them

sections.instruction: Try passing your name as a query parameter. The server will greet you by name.
sections.hint: The ? and everything after it is the query string

onSuccess.message: The server read the `name` parameter and used it in the response. Change `Sara` to your own name and run it again — the response changes dynamically. This is how search, filtering, and personalization work on every website.

sections.instruction: Now add a second parameter. Multiple parameters are separated by `&` (the 'and' sign).
sections.hint: Wrap the URL in quotes when it has & — the shell would otherwise treat & as a background operator

onSuccess.message:
`Hola, Mike!` — the server read both `name=Mike` and `lang=es` and combined them.

Note: the URL was quoted. In the shell, `&` without quotes runs a command in the background. When your URL has `&`, always wrap it in quotes.

sections.question: How do you separate multiple query parameters in a URL?
sections.explanation: Multiple query parameters are separated by the & (ampersand) symbol. For example: ?name=Mike&lang=es sends two parameters in a single URL.

sections.prompt: Query parameters start with a _____ symbol after the URL path.

sections.instruction: Query parameters also work for pagination — limiting how many results you get back.
sections.prompt: Type `curl https://mock.zero2claude.dev/items?limit=3` to get only 3 items instead of all 10.
sections.hint: Add ?limit=3 at the end of the items URL

onSuccess.message: Without `?limit=3`, the server returns all 10 items. With it, only 3. This is called **pagination** — controlling how much data you get per request. Every large API uses it. Without it, a single request to a popular API could return millions of records.

completionMessage: Query parameters are how you filter, search, sort, and paginate API responses. Now you can customize any request just by changing the URL.
